item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I love this. This reminds me a bit of the Turrets from Portal, and that's a good thing. Not to say that this isn't completely disturbing, either. I might have a hard time sleeping tonight.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dont know why i never thought I'd be sad about a missile system, but hey. Thats what scps are for

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yet another example of comment I wish I can like but I couldn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't tell what creeps me more. This or Sons of the Nation.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Teenage AI Suicide Bombers for guidance chip systems, you say? A neat idea that gets played off with much tragedy here.

With respect to ARD's comment on this matching with the MO of Anderson Robotics, I feel that given the existence of such things as SCP-2306, this kind of thing is right up the alley for some of the more morally dubious developers hired by the company. So A+ for exploring that territory.

+1. Good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Every missile is self-contained - there is no way to directly interact with them. They're just trapped in their own private jingoistic universe. The last logs are debug information, parsed with anomalous technology - it's not something anyone was supposed to see.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On one hand, I feel like the adolescent missiles weren't really well-characterized; I personally never felt bad for them because I didn't really connect with them — I was just told they were adolescents and then got a brief dialogue snippet of their final moments. It might have connected more deeply with me if I had a chance to see them interacting with each other or gotten a sense of who they were before those moments.

On the other hand, you basically wrote Ender's Game except with sapient missiles. On that basis alone, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That was absurdly dark and depressing. Just, damn. Solid job at a short but repetitively gut-punching article without dragging on for too long.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sentient missile guidance systems weren't exactly something I expected to bring me to near-tears today. That was really emotional and thought-provoking. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This reminded me of "Dark Star", only darker. Using AI as a weapons guidance system seems like using a Faberge Easter egg as a hammer, which just highlights how coldly the AIs are viewed by their creators.

There's a whole constellation of misery here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You made me care about an AI, and that's no small feat. +1 for tight presentation without dragging it out or descending into narmy angst-levels.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"What is my purpose?"

"You guide missiles."

"Oh my god."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gods, that hits hard. +1, and now I'm going to cry myself to sleep.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2873 refers to a missile guidance assembly manufactured by Anderson Robotics, apparently a derivative work of [REDACTED] resulting in a less effective but highly miniaturized artificial intelligence system-on-a-chip.

I had to mentally ready my feels for this. ;<

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean, if the Foundation isn't the place to see preteen AIs being indoctrinated into becoming child suicide bombers and encountering the horrific pointlessness of their existence in the moments before their death, I don't know what is. Jesus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Jesus. This hit me in the feels in a way I wasn't really expecting. Poor kids.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have my doubts on whether or not Anderson Robotics entering the field of guided missiles is in line with the GoI, but I'm willing to overlook that on the sheer strength of the morbid humor of the piece. The tragedy of indoctrinating what are essentially preteens into becoming suicide bombers and their subsequent brief flashes of insight before death goes so far that it wraps all the way around from deep tragedy to wicked comedy. It doesn't deliver any meaningful political insights, but it doesn't need to.

item.title: SCP-2873: Another Human Weapon
item.content:encoded:
Companion piece for SCP-3033. Why strip people of their autonomy to make them weapons when you can grant weapons free will?

Thanks to GreenWolf, Decibelle, ARD, Dexanote, fieldstone, and everyone else that took a look at this in its drafting period.
